query: morphology and functions of rough endoplasmic reticulum including its membrane structure and ribosome attachment

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a structure found within the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells. Its composition has two components: the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) and the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). The SER is generally used for the creation/ storage of lipids and steroids, while the RER plays a significant role in the synthesis of various proteins. The RER is termed "rough" due ribosomal attachments to the surface compared to the SER, which does not have ribosomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The RER is morphologically distinguishable by its series of convoluted, flattened like membrane sheets (called cisternae) that arise near the nucleus and extend across the cytoplasm. Sections of the cisternae contain ribosomes, held together by microtubules of the cytoskeleton. Changes in the pattern of microtubule polymerization lead to a change in RER morphology. Furthermore, the ribosomes of the RER are not permanently attached to the membrane. They constantly attach and detach to the membrane as needed for protein synthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Edges of the ER sheets tend to have a degree of high-curvature that require stabilization. Proteins that help with this stabilization are reticulons and DP1/Yop1p. These integral membrane proteins contribute to the curvature by forming a transmembrane hairpin that acts as a wedge. [1] This protein made wedge displaces lipids in the outer leaflet of the bilayer, which further creates the curvature of the ER membrane. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Microscopy, Light -- Endoplasmic Reticulum
retrieved_docs.content: The rER has membranes with a rough outer surface due to the presence of ribosomes. This organelle functions as an internal transport system for the cell, facilitating the movement of synthesized proteins. The rER lumen is continuous with that of the smooth endoplasmic reticulum and the perinuclear space. This structure primarily modifies synthesized proteins used by the cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The RER is associated with many roles in protein synthesis, which also include post-translational modifications, folding, and sorting. Membrane-bound ribosomes in the RER translate the mature mRNA transcript into amino acids that are attached to become polypeptides. Eukaryotic ribosomes (80S) consist of two unequal subunits: the small subunit (40S) and the large subunit (60S). The (S) refers to a Svedberg unit, which is a non-metric unit of measure for the sedimentation rate. Each Svedberg unit is equal to 10^-13 seconds. After protein synthesis, the post-translational addition of some carbohydrates and some proteolytic cleavages occur in the RER. However, most post-translational modifications take place in the Golgi complex.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry -- Histochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: Since the outer membrane of the RER is studded with ribosomes, it will have a relatively basophilic stain in the cytoplasm when using H&E stains, due to the presence of negatively charged rRNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Microscopy, Light -- Endoplasmic Reticulum
retrieved_docs.content: The sER consists of membranes with a smooth outer surface and lacks ribosomes. This organelle is responsible for lipid production and further processing of membrane proteins synthesized by the rER. The sER also aids in drug detoxification.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Microscopy, Light -- Endoplasmic Reticulum
retrieved_docs.content: The endoplasmic reticulum exists in 2 forms: rough and smooth. The rER is covered with ribosomes and plays a role in protein synthesis. The smooth endoplasmic reticulum (sER) is involved in the production of specific lipids and carbohydrates.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The first step towards proper folding requires oligosaccharyltransferases to glycosylate the newly formed peptide chain. Glycosylation of the peptide increases its solubility and protects it until chaperones can bind to the chain and begin the folding process. Major chaperones in this process include Calnexin (CNX), Calreticulin (CRT), and binding immunoglobulin protein (BiP). [2] [3] The RER also consists of enzymes that catalyze the formation of disulfide bonds necessary for a protein's tertiary and quaternary structure. If the protein does not fold correctly, the molecular chaperons rebind onto the polypeptide and attempt to fold the protein into the correct shape. After multiple failed attempts, the misfolded proteins are exported to the cytosol and degraded.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry -- Cytochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: A cytochemical study revealed that BiP is found exclusively within the RER. [5] In mammalian cells specifically, ribophorin I was found to be an RER-specific protein as well. [6] Ribosomes, along with their subunits, are a characteristic of the RER and can help identify it. However, these proteins are also found free-floating throughout the cytoplasm and other parts of the cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Microscopy, Electron
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the electron micrograph section, it can show a detailed view of the RER, which includes cisterna, microtubules, ribosomes, and surrounding structures. Eukaryotic cisterna and tubules have a diameter of 30 to 50 nm, while ribosomes are 25 to 30 nm. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: After proper synthesis and folding of the protein, it goes to the edges of the RER. The vesicular coat protein complex II (COPII) mediates the formation of vesicles at the RER edges, which transport the protein product towards the Golgi apparatus for further processing. [4] Protein products that must stay within the ER move through retrograde transport from the Golgi, using vesicles formed by coat protein complex I (COPI). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Microscopy, Light -- Ribosomes
retrieved_docs.content: Ribosomes measure approximately 15 nm in diameter and exist in both attached and free, unbound forms. Many are tethered to the rough endoplasmic reticulum (rER), while others are dispersed throughout the cytoplasm. When found individually, these organelles are referred to as "monosomes," whereas groups of ribosomes are called "polyribosomes." Each ribosome consists of subunits made of RNA, which may be ribosomal (rRNA), messenger (mRNA), or transfer (tRNA) RNA. Ribosomes are responsible for synthesizing proteins used both inside and outside the cytoplasm, making them the cell’s protein factory. The role of these structures is to “translate” genetic instructions from DNA into functional proteins. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: The detailed structure of the RER can't be effectively studied through light microscopy. However, there is an abundance of membrane-bound ribosomes within the RER, making those specific areas extremely basophilic and visible. Visibility of the RER increases in cells that are highly secretory such as Nissl substance in neurons and exocrine pancreatic acinar cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Unfolded or misfolded proteins remain in the ER, translocated to the cytoplasm by ER-associated protein degradation (ERAD), and then degraded by the proteasome. The amount of unfolded proteins can accumulate to the extent where it exceeds the capacity of ERAD machinery to dispose of it all. This accumulation leads to ER stress in the cell. Three pathways that regulate the mammalian ER stress response are PERK (translational attenuation), ATF6 (enhanced expression of ER chaperones), and IRE1 (enhanced expression of ERAD components). [8] Collectively, these pathways work to reduce the amount of unfolded proteins within the ER.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean section is clinically associated with the low transverse cesarean section technique, and the low transverse cesarean section is in turn clinically associated with early membrane rupture (تمزق الأغشية الباكر). This relationship highlights that the specific low transverse incision used during a cesarean delivery may be linked to an increased likelihood of premature rupture of the fetal membranes, a factor that clinicians consider when planning surgical approaches and managing obstetric risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with cesarean section. Cesarean section is clinically associated with low transverse cesarean section, a common uterine incision technique. Low transverse cesarean section is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes). Recognizing that an unknown‑type cesarean scar relates to the cesarean procedure, that the procedure often involves a low transverse incision, and that this incision type is linked to premature rupture of membranes helps clinicians anticipate scar‑related issues, plan surgical approaches, and monitor for membrane rupture in patients with current or prior cesarean deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2135
retrieved_docs.content: Sweeping of membrane is directly related to the procedure known as artificial rupture of membranes to assist delivery, a technique used to promote labor. The artificial rupture of membranes to assist delivery is performed on the amnion, making the amnion the specific anatomical site where this intervention is applied.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network of pelvic‑organ abnormalities, cervical pathology, obstetric procedures, and hormonal agents that together define the assessment and management of pregnancy and labor. Abnormalities such as uterine scar, cervical cerclage suture, patulous cervical os, cervical cicatrix, cervical polyp, previous cervical operation, rigid cervix, and cervical tumor are recorded as findings located in the uterus or cervix uteri, and they may influence subsequent obstetric interventions. The cervix uteri serves as the anatomic site for numerous procedures, including obstetrical trachelotomy, artificial rupture of membranes (AROM) and membrane sweeping, induction of labor by cervical dilation, dilatation/incision of the cervix for delivery aid, and cervical incisions (عملية في عنق الرحم). Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

Clinically, low transverse cesarean section is associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر, suggesting that the occurrence of premature rupture of membranes may increase the likelihood of requiring a low transverse cesarean delivery. Together, these relationships underscore how fetal disorders and premature rupture of membranes contribute to the spectrum of pregnancy complications and influence the decision for surgical birth interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2094
retrieved_docs.content: The amnion is identified as the direct procedural site for artificial rupture of membranes, a technique performed to assist delivery. The English description “artificial rupture of membranes to assist delivery” is directly mapped from the Arabic term “تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي,” indicating that both phrases refer to the same obstetric intervention. By recognizing that the amnion is the specific structure targeted during this procedure, clinicians can better understand the mechanics of intentionally rupturing the amniotic sac to facilitate labor and apply the technique safely in patient care.
